item.title: Labour problems
item.description:
First visit to the hospital
Doctors did the test it was not water break
We are like ok no problem it’s understandable
Contractions are starting to escalate
Second time going to the hospital
Just to take off the edge
We are 48 hours in, so we went for another check up for 3rd visit
It also gave my wife just 30 minutes of sleep but we still got sent to home
Wife is in pain for next 12 hours
Doctor was very understandable
Finally
I did not know that epidural is a complicated procedure and has to be done by anesthesiologist, and omg thank you for that my wife was able to take a solid 3 hours of sleep after 3 days of pain
We are moving to OxyContin
That was supposed to induce the full dilation it did not and we stack at 6CM
Doctor’s only next choose is C-section
Yeah science baby
That was quick I did not expected that
My heart was racing on the way to see the baby ,it’s like you know it’s the love of your life and your just about to meet her
And here she is
Hi I’m Adeline
This is my dad
And this is me chilling in my moms hands

item.title: I swear I left it RIGHT. HERE!
item.description:
This house had been with my family for ... well 6 months. But today it is no more. It was burnt down. By the fire department. After we told them to. Short story and some photos I took the last few days. Come and see! Note, it's pretty long and I really don't say anything so feel free to scroll past! Some fun pics tho I hope.
So we bought this POS in the summer, and it was NOT for the house currently on the lot. Most of it was constructed without a permit and it is in POOR shape now either way. We live in fire prone northern CA so we contacted the local fire department and asked if they'd like to "assist" with the demo. They graciously accepted as these sort of training exercises are becoming more rare. Here, they've already come around and taken out the windows (going to be our greenhouse!) and reinforced the interior walls in order to do multiple burns inside for different crews to rotate through.
An example of one of the inside rooms. Some flammable/smoking material, plus obstacles where they trained crews on search and rescue in a smoke-filled environment (but not the actual burn). This happened throughout the week... but not much to see, so on to the burn we go!
We arrived the night before and pitched a tent adjacent to the house so we'd be there bright and early to greet the firepeople. BEAUTIFUL sunset and stars. This was taken immediately prior to the glowstick dance party with the in-laws and kiddos.
The morning of the burn - was chilly last night and the fog turned to a fine patina of ice. My toes are cold, but it's definitely the calm before the storm.
Uh oh. They're starting to arrive. The house is giving me some side-eye glances. I say it's ok. I lie. It's not.
And... still coming. Sorry neighbors! But we're gunna have a well trained group of local firefighters!
A lot of prep, discussion, teaching moments etc. A ton of the neighbors have shown up, wandered around, and left already bored. SOON!
And here we go! Table will have lunches soon - this is our contribution. They burn, we set out sandwiches. Odd economy we've got going to be sure.
It starts chill enough. Less experienced get their reps first while it's still a fairly fresh environment.
Activity picks up quickly. Our local FD estimated about 50 different folk were involved, about 3-4 separate departments. Party at our house- first we've had since the start of the pandemic.
Opposite side of the house - you can see the ramshackle nature of it a bit better. Yeah, sorry house, you gotta go. And it's quickly headed in that direction!
Progressing well - much more smoke now as more rooms are burned and reburned and they make their way through the structure.
Guess they're fighting in the back of the house now.
Things starting to intensify. Fire now clearly visible, smoke emanating from points all along the structure.
More crews getting their reps.
Sorry house, it's gunna be a rough day.
A new pope?
I think they're just about done at this point, presumably getting fairly unsafe inside the structure.
This is about the last shot (from the back) before the flames REALLY started jumping.
Very shortly after.
Ummm... guys. Psst! BEHIND YOU!
Apologies to the local air quality board (and neighbors). We got a pile of inspections and permits to make this magic happen.
Maybe an hour or two in? Something like that at least. Rip-roaring at this point.
Cool shot from the back. The house is not amused.
And the front. Now that's what I call a campfire!
Flame coming from every room now.
Looking through the center of the house (from the back)
Starting to fall apart/implode now.
Another shot from the road. A LOT of people stopping to chat now, I'll tell ya! Met most of the neighborhood!
Getting there.
Really not much left at this point.
This one just came out great.
Debrief and after action. The crews were fantastic. Really interesting to watch. It was an odd scene with all kinds of people casually wandering around, grabbing sandwiches etc with the inferno of my house RIGHT BEHIND THEM. Additionally I was astounded how many came up and thanked us. Are you kidding? We didn't do a darn thing! Thanks so much for doing the tough stuff for the community. Big shout out to, well, I was going to say our local crews but really, all y'all!
And back full circle. Now the real work begins... after we get more darn permits. It'll be a while yet til we actually settle here. Hope you enjoyed our little Sunday camping trip! *EDIT* Wow, most viral. Thanks all, it's been fun reading through all the positive comments. A few people have asked what the plans are and for other updates. I'll see what I can do but it's going to be a while. At least a year or so and I doubt the pics will be that entertaining. I'll try to document what I can tho!
item.dc:creator: DrOrogen

item.title: "Put your own mask on first" (Please take care of you)
item.description: Art by Lena Dirscherl lenadirscherl.com lenadirscherl.tumblr.com https://www.instagram.com/bopolena/ The title of the post is taken from all airlines in the world. Ever notice how few things we agree on as one human race? This is one of few. Your chance of taking care of someone else is reduced or eliminated unless you take care of you first. You can argue, make scenarios, deny the case studies ... but you're not going to change the facts of human psychology. It doesn't mean being selfish and ignoring others. Just a question of an order of sequence. Steps. ---------------------------------------- Today's Internet message: Men - Stop worrying every minute about how you look compared to some perfect societal image. You're okay right now! If you want to improve, set your own reasonable goals based on what YOU want. Set goals healthwise, your own appearance standards and a partner who respects you!! Often it's best to get help if you're always looking for a diet or workout to "make you feel better" about yourself. It's worse if someone in a relationship of any kind is trying to make you fit an image THEY have of what you should be. I encourage you to seek a professional immediately. Self diagnosis is generally not a good thing. I tried it for 10+ years. Didn't work. This website can help you find someone near you to talk diagnoses: https://www.psychologytoday.com/ [ I have zero association with psychologytoday or any other company or advertising scheme. I'm a semi-retired engineering manager. Just an Imgurian like you. ] Think of it like going to Urgent Care or Emergency Room if you have a serious cut or cough. A professional will tell you next steps - do not self diagnose. If you have a more serious mental health issue, call 911 (US) or message the people at places in this post: https://imgur.com/gallery/cAppClq

item.title: A little treat, for you all

item.title: The Long Walk
item.description: The Navajo Long Walk to Bosque Redondo was meant as a stopover on the way to being removed to Indian Territory. Instead, the people were concentrated at the camp with poor water and little food for four years, enduring the unspeakable. The 1868 Treaty between the Navajo Nation and the United States took place after the 1864 Sand Creek Massacre of Cheyenne and Arapaho People by Colorado Volunteers (and three federal investigations, where Army officers and other witnesses testified against the murders and mutilations by the Col. Vols.) and other heinous assaults on Native Peoples necessitated the establishment of the U.S. Peace Commission to investigate atrocities. Two Army officers were assigned as Treaty Commissioners to negotiate terms with the Navajo Treatymakers. One of the Army officers was committed to carrying our Navajo removal to Indian Territory, while the other was not committed to removal and was sympathetic to those who had suffered deprivations. The Navajo negotiators were committed to returning home and to providing for schools and doctors, which won the day. Alas, two other Long Walks ensued (most walking straight back home, while the others were paraded through Santa Fe), even though they could have been transported by train, in both 1864 and in 1868. The difference in the latter Long Walks was stark: in 1864, they were displaced prisoners with an uncertain future; in 1868, they were going home. Appreciation to Dr. Jennifer Denetdale, Dr. Manley A. Begay and Hon. Peterson Zah for researching and writing and speaking so eloquently about this history (and to Diane Enos for sharing this Long Walk map and post). This history is in the “Nation to Nation” Treaties exhibition I curated (and the accompanying book of the same title, which I got to edit) for the Smithsonian’ National Museum of the American Indian: SI-NMAI Museum on the Mall (2014-2025). Jennifer Denetdale.
item.dc:creator: mfrybeasley

item.title: unbelievable
item.description: Saw this on an internet and first thought was it was some sort of propganda or click bait. Turns out it's true. Fucking disgusting. I hope this somehow leads to change www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-politics/insuluin-prices-diabetes-alec-smith-b1972475.html%3famp Edit Some results from the T1 international survey for out of pocket costs. It is the most extensive survey available however still has a fairly small number of participants. https://imgur.com/gallery/9DuDWmV Full publication https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168822721003557
